title: INTL 210 - Introduction to International Studies
description: Introduction to the multi-disciplinary field of International Studies, with preparation for further coursework in the major. Examines development of modern world through diverse analytical lenses.

title: INTL 220 - Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: Topics studied include culture and imperialism/ cultural imperialism; orientalism and the politics of representation; (post) colonialism; cultural appropriation, hybridity, and syncretism; diasporic, transnational, cosmopolitan, and border cultures; “global” pop culture.

title: INTL 320 - Career Workshop
description: Career planning, networking, resume, cover letter, internship and job search, working a room.

title: INTL 387 - International Education Colloquium
description:
Earn credit by attending International Education Week events and participating in an online discussion forum. Mandatory pre-event meeting.
Cross-listed with: ANTH 387, COMM 387, ECON 387, GEOG 387, HIST 387, PSCI 387

title: INTL 410W - Global Issues Analysis
description: Interdisciplinary analysis of global issues bridging the perspectives of politics, economics, and culture with an emphasis on research and writing skills in the service of critical analysis.

title: INTL 490 - International Studies Capstone
description: Students synthesize and apply the student learning outcomes in the program in preparation for career or graduate studies.

title: ECON 305 - International Economics and Globalization
description: Economic theories of trade and finance. Evaluate effects of world trading system and globalization. Debate role of international institutions (WTO & IMF). Case studies on free trade areas, financial crises, protectionist policies, and labor/environmental issues. Economics and business administration majors MUST co-enroll in ECON 305D.

title: ECON 306W - Economics of the Developing World
description: Explore economic theory underlying development policies. Evaluate World Bank and IMF policy. Case studies covering poverty, inequality, trade and growth policy, debt issues, health, education, population, sustainable development, women in agriculture. Economics and business administration majors MUST co-enroll in ECON 306D.

title: PSCI 240 - Introduction to International Relations
description: Examination of institutional, economic, security, and environmental relations between and among nations. Basic concepts, theory and methods of the subfield. Required for political science majors.

title: GEOG 363 - Political Geography
description: World survey of spatial variation and interrelationships of political phenomena within a political region.

title: ANTH 318 - Ethnography
description: Problems and techniques of describing culture and representing the “other.” Critical look at the process and politics of descriptions anthropologists craft.

title: CRGS 390W - Theory and Methods
description: Introduces critical social theories informing Ethnic, Women’s and Multicultural Queer Studies. Explores workings of power, construction of the subject, dynamics of resistance, and conceptual bases of liberation movements. Emphasis rotates.

title: GEOG 311W - Geographic Research and Writing
description: Overview of discipline and profession. Use of library resources, research tools. Emphases: geographic methodologies, academic writing, presenting. Research paper and presentation on regional topic.

title: HIST 210 - Historical Methods
description: Nature of history; historical consciousness; historians’ craft; use of primary/secondary sources. Recommended first course in the major. One of four units is individualized instruction on assigned essay. History majors must take HIST 210 before taking upper division major courses.

title: ART 301 - Topics in Western Art History
description: Topics in western art history from antiquity to the present.

title: ART 301M - Topics in Western Art History Depth Experience
description: Selected topics in western art history. Explores course topics in greater detail through a combination of seminar meetings, writing assignments, and presentations.

title: ART 302 - Topics in Global Art History
description: Topics in non-western art history from antiquity to the present.

title: ART 302M - Topics in Global Art History Depth Experience
description: Selected topics in non-western art history. Explores course topics in greater detail through a combination of seminar meetings, writing assignments, and presentations.

title: ART 303 - Global Contemporary Art
description: This course explores global contemporary art and theory (post-1970). Emphasis is placed on understanding major trends as well as theoretical models so that students can generate their own informed analysis.

title: ART 303M - Global Contemporary Art Depth Experience
description: This course explores global contemporary art and theory in greater detail through a combination of seminar meetings, writing assignments, and presentations.

title: ART 304 - Topics in American Art
description: Topics in American art history, pre-contact to the present.

title: ART 304M - Topics in American Art Depth Experience
description: Topics in American art history, pre-contact to the present. Explores course topics in greater detail through a combination of seminar meetings, writing assignments, and presentations.

title: ENGL 342 - Special Topics in Shakespeare
description: Instructor selects Shakespeare plays related by genre, chronology, or theme.

title: ENGL 350 - Topics in British and Postcolonial Literatures
description: Examine specific themes, genres, historical periods, or figures. Topics vary.

title: ENGL 465C - Multicultural Issues in Literature/Languages
description:
Themes, genres, figures, theories, or movements in literary or linguistics study in relation to issues of ethnicity and/or gender. International/transnational in focus.
Cross-listed with: ES 465C, WS 465C

title: FREN 311 - French V and Stories from the Francophone World
description: Intensive reexamination of French grammar and usage in Francophone texts. Techniques and terminology of literary and cultural criticism; Aural/oral, reading and composition practice analyzing diverse literary and cultural issues.

title: FREN 312 - French VI and (R)evolution in Modern French Literature
description: Intensive reexamination of French grammar. Analysis of cultural and literary (r)evolution in modern French literary works, from the aftermath of the French Revolution to modern France. Text selections will vary.

title: FREN 480 - Upper Division Seminar/Retreat
description: Special topics seminars: Semester-long courses in language, literature or culture or shorter seminars, including creative writing, language and culture immersion courses, film seminars, retreats and international speaker series.

title: GEOG 472 - Topics in Regional Geography
description: Specialized consideration of selected world regions.

title: GERM 480 - Undergraduate Seminar
description: Film seminar, weekend language retreat, or study of a literary figure, period, or cultural aspect of Germany, Austria, or Switzerland. Also the Children’s Language Academy.

title: HIST 300 - The Era of World War I
description: Social, economic, diplomatic, political, and military background before and developments during war. Emphasis on origins and outbreak of war; total war; trench warfare; Bolshevik Revolution; peace settlement; and war’s aftermath.

title: HIST 301 - The Era of World War II
description: Social, economic, diplomatic, political, and military background before and developments during war. Emphasis on totalitarianism; appeasement; propaganda; conduct of war; civilian experiences of war; post-war settlement; beginning of Cold War.

title: HIST 345 - Imperialism
description: Study of European imperialism with emphasis on 19th/20th centuries. Exploration of details of imperialism as well as role of race, gender, mission, language, and art in shaping colonial interactions.

title: HIST 348 - Modern Germany
description: History/Historiography, 1517-present. Emphasis on ‘special path’ of German history, Lutheran Reformation, Thirty Years War, rise of Prussia, unification under Bismarck, world wars, and Germany’s role in Cold War and EU.

title: PHIL 355 - Existentialism
description: Principal existential philosophers of 19th and 20th centuries, such as Kierkegaard, Heidegger, Nietzsche, Sartre, Marcel, Buber.

title: PSCI 376 - Multilateralism and the United Nations System
description: The dynamics of multilateral diplomacy and international relations with an emphasis on the United Nations and its subsidiary and ancillary organizations.

title: PSCI 377 - Model United Nations
description: Delegate preparation for and participation in intercollegiate Model UN, emphasizing the art of lobbying, negotiation, bargaining, and international diplomacy.

title: PSCI 330 - Political Regimes and Political Change
description: Advanced study of comparative politics in regional context of Latin America, Africa, Europe, Middle East, or Asia. Topics vary; consult current class schedule.

title: SPAN 342 - Cervantes
description: Don Quixote and/ or Cervantes’ other works. His development as man and writer within the framework of his time.

title: SPAN 343 - The Golden Age
description: Spain’s greatest period of original literature: picaresque novel flourished; modern novel emerged; dramas of intrigue, history, morals, and sentiment entertained/ educated the public; poetry evolved complicated forms with conceptismo and culteranismo. Cervantes, Lope de Vega, Tirso de Molina, Calderon, Quevedo, Gongora, others.

title: SPAN 349 - Contemporary Spanish Novel
description: Tremendismo, behaviorism, alienation, ironic and social realism. Cela, Delibes, Martin Santos, Ferlosio. Relationship between the novel and political/ social conditions; problem of censorship.

title: SPAN 401 - Hispanic Civilization: Spain
description: Social, political, and cultural evolution from origins of Spanish nation to present day.
